items.text:
People often talk about qubits as though they are like bits except that somehow "they can be a zero and one all at once," whatever that means. And then they try to explain what it means by talking about flipping coins or some such. That analogy is incomplete and, worse, it's misleading. There are some similarities, though, so let's start there.


Coins have two states – heads and tails. Qubits can also be in one of two sates. These are commonly known as "zero" and "one" but in Qubit Lab we use two colors: blue and red.

So that blue circle you see on the left there? That's a qubit in the blue state. And the funny looking tank-like object next to it? We call that a flipper gate because, in analogy to flipping a coin, it acts on a qubit to change its state. Click on Show me to see how it works.

As you saw, the qubit changed to a purplish color – something in between blue and red. (For now, ignore the + sign.)  What does that mean? You see that magnifying glass? That's not an ordinary magnifying glass, it's actually a tool for measuring the state of a qubit. Click Show me to see how it works.

Whenever you measure a qubit it will always be either blue or red, never in between, just as a coin always lands on heads or tails. So if you really want to know the qubit's state you have to run that measurement more than once. Qubit Lab makes that easy to do. Once you've measured a qubit, whatever you did to it is saved and you can run the experiment again on an identical qubit by clicking on the magnifying glass. If you want to run the experiment multiple times just change the number at the bottom of the magnifying glass. Click "Show me" and I'll do both those things.
If you want to run the experiment hundreds or thousands of times, don't worry – we won't run the animation so it won't take very long!
I want to show you something really strange about qubits. You can change their state as often as you like before you measure them. You can even "unflip" them that way! Watch this!

You might have noticed that the "unflipping" process also illustrated something else: the setting on a flipper doesn't represent the final state of the qubit, it tells you how much the flipper will "rotate" the qubit from blue to red and back again. So the final state of the qubit depends on what state it started in as well as the setting on the flipper. Watch closely as I illustrate that point.

Now it's time for you to explore Qubit Lab on your own. No more "canned" experiments, but you can drag the qubit to the flipper or the magnifying glass and you can change the flipper to a different setting to see what that does to the qubit. For the time being, ignore the + and - signs. They make no difference for a single qubit. Note, though, that they only show up when the qubit is in a mixed state – they disappear when the qubit is either purely blue or red.


Happy experimenting!
Now there are two qubits, two flipper gates and a wide magnifying glass that measures both qubits at once. This is how it works.

Note that the measurement doesn't get counted until both qubits have been measured. That's because the two qubits are really one object with four possible states: blue and blue, one qubit blue and the other one red, or the other way around, or both red. That's going to be important when you explore the phenomenon known as "entanglement." 

Try running this tab with different settings on the two flipper gates. Do you see how that affects the two-qubit count? Try running qubits through those gates multiple times before making the measurement.

By the way, once you've recorded an experiment with one setting of the flipper gates you can change those settings and run the experiment again just by clicking on the magnifying glass or changing the count. You don't have to drag the qubits again.

Play around with the flipper gate settings until you are sure that you understand what they're doing to the two qubits. IMPORTANT: Just as a qubit is an object that can be in two states at once, try to think of the two qubits as though they are a single object with four states – blue-blue, blue-red, red-blue, and red-red – with different probabilities. For the moment, the qubits are independewnt of each other and the probabilities of the pair of qubits are just the product of the probabilities for each qubit, but that will change when the qubits are entangled.

Meet the controlled-not (abbreviated C-NOT) gate! As you can see, it takes two qubits. The top qubit is the control qubit, the bottom one is the target qubit. Its action is very simple. If the control qubit is blue the C-NOT doesn't change the target qubit; if the control is red the C-NOT toggles the target from blue to red or from red to blue. In either case the control qubit is unchanged.
When the control qubit is blue it doesn't change the target qubit.
But when the control is red the target qubit changes from blue to red.

As long as the control and target qubits are solid colors, either red or blue, the action of the C-NOT gate is straightforward: a blue control means "do nothing to the target" and a red one means "flip the target between red and blue." And whatever happens, the control qubit is unchanged.

What do you think would happen if the target qubit were purple – half blue and half red? Think about that before running the experiment.

Did you notice anything strange about that last experiment? No? Here are some questions to ponder:

What happens when the control qubit is purple and the target qubit is red?
What happens when they're both purple?
What if they're some other color –  closer to red or closer to blue?

Do you think you could reproduce these results using just the flipper gates?

You've seen that that innocent looking C-NOT gate can do something intereeting to the qubits when the control qubit is neither red nor blue but is in some mixed state. In that situation, even if the target qubit starts out, say, blue it ends up in a mixed state but when the two qubits are measured they're always either both blue (because a blue control leaves the blue target alone) or both red (because a red control toggles the target to red). So you never get a blue-red or a red-blue measurement.


In this situation the two qubits are said to be entangled.

When a pair of qubits is entangled it becomes a single quantum object, albeit one with four states. Classically, that statement is almost meaningless, but measuring a quantum object changes its state – in the case of a qubit, it forces it to be either red or blue. With an entangled pair we can introduce something new: we can measure just one of the qubits. Accordiing to quantum mechanics, that should change the state of the qubit pair. On this tab we've replaced the wide magnifier with a round one capable of measuring one qubit at a time. Watch closely to see what happens when we do that.

That went by pretty quickly but I hope you noticed that once you measured one of the entangled qubits the other one suddenly changed its state from purple to either red or blue. Not only that, but the second qubit matched the color of the first. Do you think that will always happen? What would have happened if we had made the target qubit red instead of blue?

Remember when you were experimenting with just one qubit how you could change its state as often as you liked before you made the measurement? You could even "unflip" the qubit and return it to its original state! What if you did the same thing with a pair of entangled qubits? What if you changed the state of one of the qubits before making the measurement? What would happen?

Here's what's happening.

Just as a single qubit can be in a mixture of two states, blue and red, an entangled pair can be in a mixture of four states: blue-blue, blue-red, red-blue, and red-red. In the experiment you just saw, the C-NOT gate put the pair into an equal mixture of blue-blue and red-red, but then the flipper gate changed one of the qubits to red and that forced the pair into the red-red condition (That's the equivalent, by the way, of what you did way back when you "unflipped" a single qubit and changed it from a purple state into red or blue and the measurement ceased to be random.)

Experiment on your own now and try to predict the outcome of each experiment before you run it. And while you're doing that think about this question:

What would happen if you sent one of your pair of qubits off to someone very far away, say on Mars? If she measured her qubit what would happen to yours? We can't do that yet in Qubit Lab but it's on the wish list (well, maybe not the Mars part!).
This tab is under construction.
